Rewritten Text: The truth is undeniable: the electoral college is an unfair, outdated, and fundamentally irrational system. Its supposed merits are largely unsupported assertions, while the counterarguments against direct elections are flimsy at best. It may be difficult to acknowledge, but Bob Dole was correct: the electoral college should be abolished. The core issue is that even the most carefully constructed defenses of the system are flawed. Many who support the electoral college fail to grasp that electors are not always trustworthy, and simply voting for an elector pledged to a particular presidential candidate offers no guarantee that the elector will actually vote as pledged. Consider the views of figures as diverse as Richard Nixon, Jimmy Carter, Bob Dole, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and the AFL-CIO: they all, at some point, advocated for the elimination of the electoral college. The fundamental desire is to move to a system where the President of the United States is chosen by popular vote. Currently, the public is not directly voting for their preferred presidential candidate; they are voting for electors. Furthermore, in the event of a tie, the election is decided by the House of Representatives, a body where citizens are not proportionally represented.
